hints: An it-cleft sentence follows the pattern: **It + be + focused element + that/who + rest of the sentence**.
Choose **was** for past events or **is** for present — match the tense of the rest of the clause.

a1.was: Correct. **Was** is the right form of 'be' in this it-cleft sentence, matching the tense of the rest of the clause.
a1.is: Incorrect. **Is** indicates present tense, but the context requires past tense (**was**).
a1.were: Incorrect. **Were** is used with plural subjects or the subjunctive. In it-cleft sentences, 'it' is singular.
a1.has been: Incorrect. **Has been** does not fit the simple-tense it-cleft pattern **It + was/is + focused element + that/who…**

hints: Emphatic **do/does/did** is used in affirmative sentences to emphasise the truth of the verb.
With a third-person singular subject in the present tense, use **does** + base form of the verb.

a1.does: Correct. Emphatic **does** adds emphasis to the verb in this present-tense affirmative sentence.
a1.do: Incorrect. **Do** does not match the subject or tense of this sentence — the correct emphatic auxiliary is **does**.
a1.is: Incorrect. **Is** is a linking verb, not an emphatic auxiliary. Use **does** to emphasise the main verb.
a1.has: Incorrect. **Has** is a perfect-tense auxiliary, not an emphatic auxiliary. Use **does** here.

hints: An it-cleft sentence follows the pattern: **It + be + focused element + that/who + rest of the sentence**.
Choose **was** for past events or **is** for present — match the tense of the rest of the clause.

a1.was: Correct. **Was** is the right form of 'be' in this it-cleft sentence, matching the tense of the rest of the clause.
a1.is: Incorrect. **Is** indicates present tense, but the context requires past tense (**was**).
a1.were: Incorrect. **Were** is used with plural subjects or the subjunctive. In it-cleft sentences, 'it' is singular.
a1.be: Incorrect. **Be** does not fit the simple-tense it-cleft pattern **It + was/is + focused element + that/who…**

hints: Emphatic **do/does/did** is used in affirmative sentences to emphasise the truth of the verb.
With a third-person singular subject in the present tense, use **does** + base form of the verb.

a1.does: Correct. Emphatic **does** adds emphasis to the verb in this present-tense affirmative sentence.
a1.do: Incorrect. **Do** does not match the subject or tense of this sentence — the correct emphatic auxiliary is **does**.
a1.is: Incorrect. **Is** is a linking verb, not an emphatic auxiliary. Use **does** to emphasise the main verb.
a1.was: Incorrect. **Was** is a linking verb, not an emphatic auxiliary. Use **does** to emphasise the main verb.

hints: In it-cleft sentences, use **who** when the focused element is a person.
Look at the word before the gap: if it names a person, **who** is the correct choice.

a1.who: Correct. **Who** is used in it-cleft sentences when the focused element is a person.
a1.that: Incorrect. While **that** is sometimes possible, **who** is the natural choice when the focused element is a person.
a1.which: Incorrect. **Which** is not the standard connector in it-cleft sentences — use **that** (or **who** for people).
a1.whose: Incorrect. **Whose** indicates possession, which is not the relationship expressed here.

hints: In it-cleft sentences, **that** is the standard connector linking the focused element to the rest of the clause.
When the focused element is not a person (a thing, time, or place), use **that**.

a1.that: Correct. **That** is the standard relative connector in it-cleft sentences for non-personal referents.
a1.which: Incorrect. **Which** is not the standard connector in it-cleft sentences — use **that** (or **who** for people).
a1.who: Incorrect. **Who** is for people, but the focused element here is not a person — use **that**.
a1.whose: Incorrect. **Whose** indicates possession, which is not the relationship expressed here.

hints: Emphatic **do** is used with I/we/you/they in the present tense for emphasis.
After emphatic **do**, the main verb stays in its base (infinitive) form.

a1.do: Correct. Emphatic **do** adds emphasis to the verb in this present-tense affirmative sentence.
a1.does: Incorrect. **Does** does not match the subject or tense of this sentence — the correct emphatic auxiliary is **do**.
a1.did: Incorrect. **Did** does not match the subject or tense of this sentence — the correct emphatic auxiliary is **do**.
a1.am: Incorrect. **Am** is a linking verb, not an emphatic auxiliary. Use **do** to emphasise the main verb.

hints: When a negative adverb such as **Never** begins a sentence, subject–auxiliary inversion is required.
Identify the tense to choose the correct auxiliary: **have/has** for present perfect, **had** for past perfect, **do/does/did** for simple tenses.

a1.have: Correct. After the negative adverb at the start, **have** is placed before the subject as required by inversion.
a1.had: Incorrect. **Had** does not match the tense or structure required after the negative adverb — the correct auxiliary is **have**.
a1.was: Incorrect. **Was** does not match the tense or structure required after the negative adverb — the correct auxiliary is **have**.
a1.did: Incorrect. **Did** does not match the tense or structure required after the negative adverb — the correct auxiliary is **have**.

Identify the tense to choose the correct auxiliary: **have/has** for present perfect, **had** for past perfect, **do/does/did** for simple tenses.

a1.do: Correct. After the negative adverb at the start, **do** is placed before the subject as required by inversion.
a1.did: Incorrect. **Did** does not match the tense or structure required after the negative adverb — the correct auxiliary is **do**.
a1.have: Incorrect. **Have** does not match the tense or structure required after the negative adverb — the correct auxiliary is **do**.
a1.does: Incorrect. **Does** does not match the tense or structure required after the negative adverb — the correct auxiliary is **do**.

hints: In it-cleft sentences, **that** is the standard connector linking the focused element to the rest of the clause.
When the focused element is not a person (a thing, time, or place), use **that**.

a1.that: Correct. **That** is the standard relative connector in it-cleft sentences for non-personal referents.
a1.which: Incorrect. **Which** is not the standard connector in it-cleft sentences — use **that** (or **who** for people).
a1.when: Incorrect. **When** is a time adverb; in it-cleft sentences the connector is **that**, even for time references.
a1.where: Incorrect. **Where** is a place adverb; it-cleft sentences use **that** as the connector.

hints: A what-cleft sentence follows the pattern: **What + clause + was/were + focused element**.
Use past tense (**was/were**) when the sentence describes a past situation.

a1.was: Correct. **Was** is the appropriate linking verb in this what-cleft sentence, matching the tense and number.
a1.were: Incorrect. **Were** does not match the tense or number required in this what-cleft sentence — use **was**.
a1.is: Incorrect. **Is** does not match the tense or number required in this what-cleft sentence — use **was**.
a1.are: Incorrect. **Are** does not match the tense or number required in this what-cleft sentence — use **was**.

hints: After **Not only** at the start of a sentence, inversion occurs: **Not only + did + subject + base verb…**
The pair **Not only…but also** is used for emphatic contrast; the first clause triggers inversion.

a1.did: Correct. **Not only did + subject + base verb** is the required inversion pattern in past-tense sentences.
a1.does: Incorrect. **Does** does not fit the **Not only did…but also** pattern required here.
a1.has: Incorrect. **Has** does not fit the **Not only did…but also** pattern required here.
a1.had: Incorrect. **Had** does not fit the **Not only did…but also** pattern required here.

After emphatic **do**, the main verb stays in its base (infinitive) form.

a1.do: Correct. Emphatic **do** adds emphasis to the verb in this present-tense affirmative sentence.
a1.does: Incorrect. **Does** does not match the subject or tense of this sentence — the correct emphatic auxiliary is **do**.

With a third-person singular subject in the present tense, use **does** + base form of the verb.

The pair **Not only…but also** is used for emphatic contrast; the first clause triggers inversion.

a1.has: Incorrect. **Has** does not fit the **Not only did…but also** pattern required here.
